summary:
The Treaty of Zsitvatorok (November 11, 1606 AD) ended the Long Turkish War (1593–1606) between the [[event:2496|Habsburg Empire]] and the [[event:2400|Ottoman Empire]] and marked a subtle but significant diplomatic shift in the relationship between the two powers — the first peace treaty negotiated as an agreement between equals rather than as a tributary arrangement. In previous Ottoman-Habsburg settlements, the Habsburg emperor had paid an annual 'gift' (in Ottoman eyes, tribute) to the sultan. The Treaty of Zsitvatorok replaced this with a single lump-sum payment and recognized the Habsburg emperor as equal in title to the Ottoman sultan — a symbolic concession reflecting the Long War's military inconclusive outcome.

The diplomatic significance was greater than the territorial changes: the treaty essentially confirmed the status quo, with neither side gaining significant territory. But the Ottoman acceptance of Habsburg equality was a notable departure from the imperial ideology that had classified Christian rulers as tributary subjects of the Sublime Porte. The Long Turkish War had demonstrated that the Ottoman military was no longer systematically superior to European forces — Habsburg armies, particularly with the help of engineers using modern siege techniques, had captured Esztergom and other fortresses that seemed impregnable. The Treaty of Zsitvatorok thus represents an early marker of the gradual Ottoman military-diplomatic retreat from Central Europe that would culminate in the [[event:2468|Treaty of Karlowitz]] (1699) a century later.
